void generateCombinations(string chars, int start, int remaining, string current, vector<string>& result) {
    if (remaining == 0) {
        result.push_back(current);
        return;

        current += chars[i];
        generateCombinations(chars, i+1, remaining-1, current, result);
        current.pop_back();
    }
}

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <string>

using namespace std;

class CombinationIterator {
public:
    CombinationIterator(string characters, int combinationLength) {
        generateCombinations(characters, combinationLength);
    }
    
    string next() {
        string res = combinations[index];
        index++;
        return res;
    }
    
    bool hasNext() {
        return index < combinations.size();
    }
    
private:
    vector<string> combinations;
    int index = 0;
    
    void generateCombinations(string chars, int k) {
        vector<string> result;
        backtrack(chars, 0, k, "", result);
        combinations = result;
    }
    
    void backtrack(string& chars, int start, int remaining, string current, vector<string>& result) {
        if (remaining == 0) {
            result.push_back(current);
            return;
        }
        for (int i = start; i <= chars.size() - remaining; ++i) {
            current += chars[i];
            backtrack(chars, i + 1, remaining - 1, current, result);
            current.pop_back();
        }
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(C(n, k))
space complexity: O(C(n, k) * k)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution precomputes all combinations during initialization and stores them in a list, allowing next() and hasNext() to operate in constant time. This approach efficiently handles the problem constraints and ensures minimal computational overhead during each method call.
